---
title: "The What, Why, and How of (Mixed) Digital Methods"
subtitle: "Digital Methods Lecture 1"
author: "Hjalmar Bang Carlsen"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme:
      - default
      - lecture_01_quarto_test.scss
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    transition: slide
    center: false
    preview-links: auto
    footer: "Digital Methods, Spring 2026"
    chalkboard: false
    navigation-mode: linear
editor: visual
---

## {.center}

::: {.deck-frame .center-stack}
::: {.section-mark}
Lecture 01
:::

# Digital methods become useful when traces become research questions

::: {.lead}
This lecture frames the course around one practical idea: how to turn digital traces into a defensible mixed-methods project.
:::

::: {.small}
Course responsible: Hjalmar Bang Carlsen, Associate Professor, SODAS
:::
:::

## Team support makes the course work {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.eyebrow}
Teaching team
:::

::: {.mini-grid}
::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Julie Hvidsteen</div>
hsl448@sodas.ku.dk
:::

::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Csilla Duray</div>
cd@plen.ku.dk
:::

::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Mihaela Capriean</div>
xbk406@sodas.ku.dk
:::
:::

::: {.card style="margin-top:0.7rem;"}
<div class="card-title">Why this matters</div>
Exercises, milestone feedback, and supervision are built around project development, not just lecture content.
:::
:::

## The lecture has four jobs {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.eyebrow}
Roadmap
:::

::: {.mini-grid}
::: {.stat-card}
::: {.number}
01
:::
<div class="card-title">Start with the end</div>
Frame the course around the final project.
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.number}
02
:::
<div class="card-title">Define digital methods</div>
Clarify what counts as digital in data, method, and analysis.
:::

::: {.stat-card}
::: {.number}
03
:::
<div class="card-title">Define mixed methods</div>
Show why digital work often needs both qual and quant moves.
:::
:::

::: {.card style="margin-top:0.7rem;"}
<div class="card-title">Final move</div>
Explain how the course is organized as a research collective that helps projects take shape.
:::
:::

## The course is built around one project {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.hero-grid}
::: {.stack}
<div class="statement-title">The exam is a 25-page mixed digital methods project</div>

::: {.support}
Students collect digital data, answer a self-chosen research question, and combine qualitative and quantitative analysis.
:::
:::

::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">The project should include</div>
- a clear research design
- a datasite and data collection strategy
- qualitative analysis
- quantitative analysis
- integration across methods
:::
:::
:::

## The exam rewards integration, not separate mini-studies {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.eyebrow}
Exam focus
:::

::: {.trace-row}
::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
◎
:::
Research design
:::

::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
◌
:::
Netnography
:::

::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
◍
:::
Qualitative text analysis
:::

::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
◐
:::
Simple quantitative analysis
:::
:::

::: {.card style="margin-top:0.8rem;"}
<div class="card-title">The key standard</div>
The strongest projects do not place qual and quant side by side. They make them inform each other.
:::
:::

## A good project has a recognisable structure {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.two-col}
::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Must-haves</div>
- datasite description informed by netnography
- data collection account
- research design
- qualitative text analysis
- quantitative text analysis
- validated computational classification
:::

::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Typical paper architecture</div>
1. Introduction  
2. Background and datasite  
3. Theory and literature  
4. Design  
5. Data collection  
6. Methods  
7. Analysis  
8. Conclusion
:::
:::
:::

## Good projects turn a concrete case into an analytical question {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.two-col}
::: {.stack}
<div class="statement-title">Example: “Incels and Extremism”</div>

::: {.support}
Research question: *What tenets in incel culture resemble the structure of extremist ideology?*
:::

::: {.small}
The point of the example is not the topic itself. It is the design logic connecting question, concept, data, and analysis.
:::
:::

::: {.image-card}
![](image-3.png)
:::
:::
:::

## A convincing project connects datasite and collection strategy {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.image-split}
::: {.image-card}
![](image-2.png)
:::

::: {.image-card}
![](image-4.png)
:::
:::
:::

## Mixed work means patterns and passages must speak to each other {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.two-col}
::: {.quote-card}
<div class="card-title">Qualitative evidence</div>
> “I am the worst of the worst. I don’t deserve to mate and breed.”

This kind of passage gives interpretive depth that a pattern alone cannot supply.
:::

::: {.image-card}
![](image-5.png)
:::
:::
:::

## Digital methods begin with digital traces {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.hero-grid}
::: {.stack}
<div class="statement-title">Digital methods repurpose platform traces as research data</div>

::: {.support}
The object is not “the internet” in general. The object is the traces, rankings, links, metadata, and interactions generated inside specific media environments.
:::
:::

::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Four examples of traces</div>
- likes and reactions
- comments and replies
- hashtags and links
- rankings, recommendations, and metrics
:::
:::
:::

## Digital methods also learn from the medium itself {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.two-col}
::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">The method point</div>
Platforms already sort, rank, recommend, cluster, and connect content.

Digital methods learn from those built-in operations instead of treating them as irrelevant noise.
:::

::: {.image-card}
![](image.png)
:::
:::
:::

## Digital methods interpret patterns rather than stopping at them {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.two-col}
::: {.image-card}
![](image-7.png)
:::

::: {.image-card}
![](image-6.png)
:::
:::

::: {.card style="margin-top:0.7rem;"}
<div class="card-title">Core move</div>
Quantitative patterns become useful when they are interpreted as medium-specific social formations.
:::
:::

## Mixed methods matter because digital data are mixed already {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.concept-grid}
::: {.stat-card}
<div class="card-title">In terms of data</div>
Digital material can be qualitative in form and quantitative in scale.
:::

::: {.stat-card}
<div class="card-title">In terms of methods</div>
Projects often need both contextual observation and scalable collection.
:::
:::

::: {.card style="margin-top:0.7rem;"}
<div class="card-title">In terms of analysis</div>
Interpretive and statistical work both become weaker when they are isolated from each other.
:::
:::

## Mixed digital methods combine three things at once {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.trace-row}
::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
01
:::
Repurpose and combine qualitative and quantitative digital traces
:::

::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
02
:::
Combine methods such as netnography and web scraping
:::

::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
03
:::
Combine interpretive and analytical forms of computer-assisted analysis
:::

::: {.trace-item}
::: {.icon}
⇄
:::
Make the three layers inform each other
:::
:::
:::

## The point is not better platform engineering {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.two-col}
::: {.stack}
<div class="statement-title">The goal is to investigate wider cultural dynamics</div>

::: {.support}
Digital methods use medium-specific operations to study public life, discourse, organization, and social worlds. They do not aim to optimize the platforms themselves.
:::
:::

::: {.quote-card}
<div class="card-title">Practical warning</div>
If a project only collects platform data without asking what those traces mean in context, it stops too early.
:::
:::
:::

## The course treats the class as a research collective {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.hero-grid}
::: {.stack}
<div class="statement-title">The lecture course is designed to help projects mature in public</div>

::: {.support}
The class works as a collective, while exercise groups function as topical clusters made up of smaller independent project teams.
:::
:::

::: {.image-card}
![](image-11.png)
:::
:::
:::

## Weekly routines keep projects moving {.smaller}

::: {.deck-frame}
::: {.two-col}
::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Exercise classes</div>
- one-slide update every Tuesday
- group work on milestones
- feedback on project development
- occasional small methods exercises
:::

::: {.card}
<div class="card-title">Supervision sessions</div>
- six sessions per group
- 25 minutes each
- short progress update plus current challenge
- focused help on design and analysis
:::
:::
:::

## The first takeaway is simple {.center}

::: {.deck-frame .center-stack}
::: {.section-mark}
Takeaway
:::

# A strong mixed digital methods project links traces, context, and interpretation

::: {.lead}
That is the standard the rest of the course will keep returning to.
:::
:::
